column1: # QBSD Export with Schema Metadata

# Query: Given a protein sequence
column2: can it be determined whether or not it contains a nuclear export signal (NES)? If it does

column1: # Schema Creation: gemini gemini-2.5-flash-lite
# Column Definitions:
# Full Name: Official protein name of the cargo protein containing the NES.
#   Rationale: Identifies the protein for which nuclear export signal (NES) is annotated
column2: enabling linking to other protein databases and literature.

column1: # Alternative Names: Other names or aliases for the same cargo protein (e.g. synonyms
#   Rationale: Provides flexibility for matching literature or databases that may refer to the same protein by different names.
# Organism: Source organism in which the NES-containing cargo protein was experimentally characterized.
#   Rationale: Important for interpreting export data and assessing conservation or species-specificity of NESs.
#   Allowed Values: Homo sapiens (Human)
# Experimental Evidence for CRM1-mediated Export: Type(s) of experimental evidence supporting CRM1-dependent nuclear export of the cargo (e.g. leptomycin B sensitivity
#   Rationale: Distinguishes validated NES cargoes from putative ones
column2: enabling confidence assessments and filtering by experimental support.

column1: #   Allowed Values: LMB Sensitive
column3: Reporter activity when co-expressed with CRM1
column4: Mislocalization in Xpo1-1 temperature sensitive strains
column5: Inhibit or inhibited by other CRM1 cargos

column1: # Mutations That Affect Nuclear Export: Reported mutations (amino-acid changes) in the cargo protein that impair or abolish its nuclear export (i.e. NES functional mutations).
#   Rationale: Identifies critical residues for export
column2: allowing mapping of functionally important positions and validation of the NES region.

column1: # Mutations That Affect CRM1 Binding: Reported mutations in the NES (or cargo) that specifically impair binding to export receptor CRM1 (without necessarily abolishing export).
#   Rationale: Provides insight into the molecular determinants of NES–CRM1 interaction
column2: useful for structure–function analyses and modeling.

column1: # Functional Export Signals: Sequence positions (start/end indices) of the experimentally validated Nuclear Export Signal within the cargo protein.
#   Rationale: Pinpoints the exact region responsible for export
# Secondary Structure of Export Signal: Structural context (e.g. predicted or known secondary structure) of the NES region (e.g. helix
#   Rationale: Provides information about conformational context of NES
column2: which affects its accessibility and binding to CRM1; helpful for structural and biophysical analyses.

column1: #   Allowed Values: α-helix
# Other Residues Important for Export: Additional residues (beyond the core NES) reported to influence export — e.g. flanking residues
#   Rationale: Captures more subtle contributions to export functionality (e.g. residues that modulate NES strength or regulation)
column2: enabling more nuanced understanding and better predictive modeling.

column1: # Sequence: Full amino-acid sequence of the cargo protein.
#   Rationale: Provides the context for the NES
# is_NesDB_doubt: A flag or indicator of quality/confidence (or doubt) regarding the validity of the NES annotation in this entry.
#   Rationale: Allows users to filter out entries with uncertain or disputed evidence
column2: improving reliability when constructing benchmark sets or training predictive models.

column20: [APC Protein_12070164_full] The conserved hydrophobic residues Leu-165, Ile-169, and Leu-172 of NES-(165–174) are buried within the coiled coil of helices I and II (Figs. 3 B and 5 B). | [APC Protein_12070164_full] The second coiled coil is formed by the five apolar interactions of the amino acids Tyr-158, Leu-162, Leu-165, Ile-169, and Leu-172 of helix I with the amino acids Arg-186, Leu-189, Ala-193, Ile-196, and Met-200 of helix II, respectively (Fig. 3 B). | [APC Protein_12070164_full] Second, the Ser to Ile mutation at position 171 is located within the NES-(165–174) (44) with the Ser side chain pointing toward the outside. | [APC Protein_12070164_full] Our finding that the software NetPhos (11) identified Ser-171 as a putative phosphorylation site supports this notion.

column9: Leptomycin B sensitivity, localization assays, mutagenesis
column10: [Actin_9501085_full] Monomeic actin, when injected into the nucleus, was rapidly exported in a manner which was sensitive to leptomycin B (LMB), a specific inhibitor of NES‐dependent nuclear export. | [Actin_9501085_full] LMB treatment of cells prevented nuclear exclusion of endogenous actin, inducing its nuclear accumulation. | [Actin_9501085_full] Furthermore, actin mutants with disrupted NESs accumulated in the nucleus. | [Actin_9501085_full] Both conjugates were almost completely excluded from the nucleus within 10 min (Figure 2A and B) whereas coinjected RITC–BSA remained in the nucleus (Figure 2A and B). Importantly, the nuclear export of both NES‐1–OV and NES‐2–OV was inhibited completely by leptomycin B (LMB) (Figure 2A and B), a specific inhibitor of nuclear export mediated by leucine‐rich NESs | [Actin_9501085_full] Thus for the most part, nuclear export of the injected actin is sensitive to LMB and therefore is most likely NES‐mediated. | [Actin_9501085_full] To confirm that the nuclear export of actin is mediated by its two NES sequences, NES‐1 and NES‐2, we made three mutant actins in which critical hydrophobic residues (the third and fourth hydrophobic residue) in either NES‐1 or NES‐2, or both were replaced by alanines to disrupt NES‐1, NES‐2 or both, respectively (disNES‐1, disNES‐2, or disNES‐1&2 in Figure 4A).
column11: Mutations disrupting NES-1 and/or NES-2 lead to nuclear accumulation.
column12: [Actin_9501085_full] Furthermore, actin mutants with disrupted NESs accumulated in the nucleus. | [Actin_9501085_full] In contrast, the mutant actin in which both NESs were disrupted (disNES‐1&2) localized primarily to the nucleus, i.e. in ∼60% of the cells the staining in the nucleus was stronger than in the cytoplasm (Figure 4B), and in ∼40% of the cells the staining intensity was almost the same in both regions. | [Actin_9501085_full] In 3Y1 cells, in which the transfected actins were expressed to a relatively higher level, even wild‐type actin could be detected in the nucleus (Figure 4C), disNES‐1 and disNES‐2 actins accumulated in the nucleus, and disNES‐1&2 actin accumulated in the nucleus more strongly (Figure 4C).
column16: [Actin_9501085_full] we found two such sequences in the middle part of the molecule. Both of these sequences are shown to have nuclear export activity. | [Actin_9501085_full] We found two putative NES sequences in actin, designated NES‐1 and NES‐2, respectively (Figure 1A). | [Actin_9501085_full] The NES‐1 peptide corresponding to residues 170–181 (ALPHAIMRLDLA) of actin and the NES‐2 peptide corresponding to residues 211–222 (DIKEKLCYVALD) of actin were synthesized
column18: [Actin_9501085_full] The NES‐1 (yellow, with the critical hydrophobic residues in green) portion and the NES‐2 (yellow, with the critical hydrophobic residues in red) portion seem to be exposed at the exterior.

column15: The paper identifies 19 novel NES motifs in 14 cancer-related proteins, and two NES motifs in human micropeptides (SPROHSA141543 and SPROHSA010409). Specific sequences are provided in Supplementary Tables S2 and S3.
column16: [CDC27_ 32882917_full] Using cellular assays, we first map 19 new sequence motifs with nuclear export activity in 14 cancer-related proteins that are potential CRM1 cargos. | [CDC27_ 32882917_full] By combining in silico prediction with nuclear export assays, we report the identification of 19 novel sequence motifs with nuclear export activity in 14 proteins (SPN90, TFE3, SHIP2, PER1, SEPT6, SIR2, UBR5, FR1OP, AP2B1, IF2B, mTOR, CRTC1, CDC27 and ZO2, see Supplementary Figure S1) that belong to a putative “XPO1/CRM1-cancer exportome”. | [CDC27_ 32882917_full] Indeed, we report here the identification of two human micropeptides (SmProt database ID SPROHSA141543 and SPROHSA010409) bearing CRM1-dependent NESs. | [CDC27_ 32882917_full] Table S2. Selected candidate NES motifs in "XPO1/CRM1-cancer exportome" proteins predicted in silico and experimentally tested using the SRVB/A assay and/or the Rev(1.4)-GFP assay. | [CDC27_ 32882917_full] Table S3. Selected in silico predicted candidate NES motifs in human micropeptides
column19: [{'value': 'K568', 'excerpt': 'These studies have also identified K568 as a key residue in CRM1 groove, [11]. This residue not only contributes to NES binding by hydrogen bonding with the NES backbone, but also appears to function as a “specificity filter” that physically blocks binding of those NES-like peptides whose structural features are not optimal for docking into the groove [11].'}, {'value': 'E571', 'excerpt': 'Importantly, K568 establishes electrostatic interactions with E571, the CRM1 residue recurrently mutated in certain hematological malignancies [18] (reviewed in [13]).'}, {'value': 'I521, L525, F561 and F572', 'excerpt': 'A series of landmark studies a decade ago showed that NES peptides dock into five hydrophobic pockets of the CRM1 groove, and identified several key amino acids, including I521, L525, F561 and F572 (residue numbering of human CRM1 is used throughout the text) that establish hydrophobic interactions with NES residues [10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17].'}]
